stimulus:
Text 1

Biologists have long puzzled over how thousands of distinct soil-microbe species can coexist within a single handful of forest soil, all drawing on the same pool of carbon and nitrogen. According to conventional wisdom, one species ought to dominate after outcompeting the rest. Why, then, do so many persist? Decades of effort have yet to produce a satisfying explanation.

Text 2

Microbial ecologist Devon Anand and colleagues have linked soil-microbe diversity to the physical structure of the soil itself. Because individual cells are so small relative to the gaps between soil grains, microbes inhabit countless tiny, semi-isolated pore spaces, each effectively functioning as its own miniature habitat. The team argues that, as a result, neighboring species rarely interact directly enough to compete for the same resources, making head-to-head competition among soil microbes far less common than ecologists had previously assumed.
stem: Based on the texts, how would Anand and colleagues (Text 2) most likely respond to the "conventional wisdom" discussed in Text 1?

answerOptions.content: By asserting that it fails to recognize that frequent nutrient inputs from decaying plant matter prevent competition between microbial species
By suggesting that their own findings help clarify how microbial species are able to compete with larger soil organisms
By arguing that it rests on a misconception about microbial species competing directly with one another
By recommending that more biologists study how competition among microbial species intensifies as soil-pore size decreases

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The conventional wisdom in Text 1 holds that one species should outcompete the rest within a diverse population. Text 2 explains that Anand and colleagues believe head-to-head competition among soil microbes is much less common than ecologists assumed, since cells inhabit semi-isolated pore spaces. So Anand and colleagues would argue the conventional wisdom misconstrues how often microbes directly compete.

Choice A is incorrect because Text 2 says nothing about nutrient inputs. Choice B is incorrect because both texts focus on competition within microbial communities, not between microbes and larger organisms. Choice D is incorrect because Text 2 implies that smaller pore size decreases, not increases, direct competition.
